answer:
**The Falsafa corpus does not contain a direct philosophical explanation or definition of *shahid* (شهید, meaning "martyr" or "witness") from Allama Iqbal's works.** Iqbal's included texts are the three parts of his Urdu poetry collection *Bang-e-Dara* (1924), and searches across translations, transliterations, and bodies yield only poetic usages, not conceptual analysis.<sup>[1]</sup><sup>[2]</sup>

### Poetic Contexts for "Witness"
- In "Himalaya" (*Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, ch. 25), a singing stream descending the mountain is described as:  
  > Aati hai nadi faraaz-e-koh se gaati hui  
  > Kausar o Tasneem ki maujon ko sharmati hui  
  > **Aaina sa shahid-e-qudrat ko dikhlaati hui**<sup>[3]</sup>  

  The translation renders this as a "mirror to the face of Nature," portraying the stream as a reflective *witness* (*shahid-e-qudrat*) to divine creation, revealing nature's beauty while navigating obstacles.<sup>[4]</sup><sup>[5]</sup> This evokes *shahid* as an observant mirror of God's signs, aligning with Sufi imagery of witnessing divine reality.

### Poetic Contexts for "Martyr"
Iqbal references *martyrs* (translated from Urdu/Arabic *shuhada*) in revolutionary or revivalist tones:
- In "Dil se jo baat nikalti hai..." (*Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, ch. 4):  
  > The garden will be cleansed of thorn and weed;  
  > **The martyrs’ blood, a life-bestowing seed**,  
  > Is painting all the flowerbeds with red.<sup>[6]</sup>  

  Here, martyrs' blood renews the Islamic garden/nation metaphorically.
- Later in the same poem: "The world that nursed your martyrs" refers to a "dark-skinned world" (likely India/Africa), tying martyrdom to global Muslim struggle.<sup>[7]</sup>
- In "Huzoor Risalat Ma'ab Mein" (*Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, ch. 11), the poet offers the Prophet a vial of "the blood of Tripoli's martyrs," symbolizing the ummah's honor amid colonial resistance (e.g., Italian invasion of Libya, 1911).<sup>[8]</sup>

These evoke *shahid* as both passive witness (to nature/divinity) and active martyr (sacrificial blood fertilizing revival). However, no passage unpacks Iqbal's full philosophy on the term—likely found in his Persian works (*Asrar-i-Khudi*, *Rumuz-i-Bekhudi*) or prose (*Reconstruction of Religious Thought in Islam*), absent from this corpus of 37 works.

<sup>[1]</sup>: *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, [ch. 25](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/25-himalaya/translation/).  
<sup>[2]</sup>: *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, [ch. 4](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/04-dil-se-jo-baat-nikalti-hai-asar-rakhti-hai/translation/), [ch. 11](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/11-huzoor-risalat-maab-mein/translation/).  
<sup>[3]</sup>: *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, ch. 25, [p-81b343](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/25-himalaya/transliteration/#p-81b343).  
<sup>[4]</sup>: *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, ch. 25, [p-87573e](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/25-himalaya/translation/#p-87573e).  
<sup>[5]</sup>: Ibid., [p-b68a16](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/25-himalaya/translation/#p-b68a16).  
<sup>[6]</sup>: *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, ch. 4, [p-169e10](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/04-dil-se-jo-baat-nikalti-hai-asar-rakhti-hai/translation/#p-169e10).  
<sup>[7]</sup>: Ibid., [p-e2b972](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/04-dil-se-jo-baat-nikalti-hai-asar-rakhti-hai/translation/#p-e2b972).  
<sup>[8]</sup>: *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, ch. 11, [p-e4e5a8](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/11-huzoor-risalat-maab-mein/translation/#p-e4e5a8).
